coredata.dc:title: Surface charge affects foliar uptake, transport and physiological effects of functionalized graphene quantum dots in plants
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Haifeng
Wang, Meng
Wang, Jing
Wang, Weipeng
coredata.dc:description: The present study focused on evaluating the effects of surface charge on foliar uptake, translocation and physiological response of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in maize (Zea mays L.) plants. Here, maize seedlings were foliar exposed to 10 mg/L GQDs modified with positively charged amino functional groups (NH2-GQDs) and negatively charged hydroxyl functional groups (OH-GQDs) for 8 days, respectively. Positively charged NH2-GQDs adhered on the cuticle layer were approximately 2.1 times more than the negatively charged OH-GQDs due to the electrostatic attraction to plant cell wall with negative charge. Within the initial 5 days, most of the GQDs internalized into the leaves via stomatal opening were efficiently translocated to the vasculature and moved down to the roots. Thereafter, the enlargement of aggregation made the particle sizes approach and even exceed the pipe diameter of vascular bundle, thus limiting the leaf-to-root translocation of GQDs, especially for NH2-GQDs. Compared with positively charged NH2-GQDs, negatively charged OH-GQDs induced stronger inhibitory effect on photosynthesis, higher accumulation of malondialdehyde and stimulation to enzyme activities of superoxide dismutase, catalase, and peroxidase. Overall, our findings provide direct evidence for the influence of surface charge on foliar uptake, translocation, and physiological effects of GQDs in crop plants, and imply that foliar exposure of GQDs negatively impact plant photosynthesis and growth health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Surface charge
Foliar uptake
Translocation
Physiological effects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of GQDs on maize leaves
Analyses of chlorophyll paraments in maize leaves
Induction of oxidation stress by GQDs
Quantification of GQDs on maize leaves
Uptake and translocation of GQDs in maize seedlings
Effect of GQDs on photosynthesis of maize seedlings
Effects of GQDs on the activity of antioxidant enzymes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence spectra of NH2-GQDs (A) and OH-GQDs (C) with increasing concentrations (0, 100, 200, 400, 500, 600, 800, 1000 ng/spot) on the maize leaf surfaces; The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of NH2-GQDs (B). and OH-GQDs (D) Each data was the mean of nine replicates.

Micro-fluorescent images of NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs on the surface of maize leaves (A). The bright white patch marked with the yellow arrow represents the fluorescence signal. The variation of the fluorescence spectra of NH2-GQDs (B) and OH-GQDs (C) on the leaf surfaces with the prolonged exposure time. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Micro-fluorescent images of Control (A), NH2-GQDs (B) and OH-GQDs (C) on the surface of maize stems and roots with the prolonged exposure time. The bright white patch marked with the yellow arrow represents the fluorescence signal. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The schematic diagram for the translocation pathway of NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs in the whole plants after deposition on the leaves.
Changes in the main chlorophyll fluorescence parameters F0 (A), Fv/Fm (B), Rfd (C), qP (D) and NPQ (E) of control group and plants upon the exposure to NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs.
Chlorophyll fluorescence images of F0, Fv/Fm and Rfd obtained from maize leaves with various treatment after 8 days: control, exposure to NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs.

Changes in MDA contents (A) of the maize leaves over the entire exposure period, and of the stems and roots at day 8. The changes in the activities of SOD, CAT and POD in the leaves of control plants and NH2-GQDs, OH-GQDs treated plants at day 2 and 8 (C). The error bar represents the standard deviation. Each presented result was the average of fifteen measurements.

head.ce:title: Surface charge affects foliar uptake, transport and physiological effects of functionalized graphene quantum dots in plants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resource, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resource, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, P.R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Environment and Resource, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study focused on evaluating the effects of surface charge on foliar uptake, translocation and physiological response of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in maize (Zea mays L.) plants. Here, maize seedlings were foliar exposed to 10 mg/L GQDs modified with positively charged amino functional groups (NH2-GQDs) and negatively charged hydroxyl functional groups (OH-GQDs) for 8 days, respectively. Positively charged NH2-GQDs adhered on the cuticle layer were approximately 2.1 times more than the negatively charged OH-GQDs due to the electrostatic attraction to plant cell wall with negative charge. Within the initial 5 days, most of the GQDs internalized into the leaves via stomatal opening were efficiently translocated to the vasculature and moved down to the roots. Thereafter, the enlargement of aggregation made the particle sizes approach and even exceed the pipe diameter of vascular bundle, thus limiting the leaf-to-root translocation of GQDs, especially for NH2-GQDs. Compared with positively charged NH2-GQDs, negatively charged OH-GQDs induced stronger inhibitory effect on photosynthesis, higher accumulation of malondialdehyde and stimulation to enzyme activities of superoxide dismutase, catalase, and peroxidase. Overall, our findings provide direct evidence for the influence of surface charge on foliar uptake, translocation, and physiological effects of GQDs in crop plants, and imply that foliar exposure of GQDs negatively impact plant photosynthesis and growth health.

ce:section.ce:para: Engineering nanoparticles (NPs) are defined as substances less than 100 nm in at least one dimension (Xiao et al., 2021), different from their corresponding bulk materials, the superior structural properties such as larger specific surface area and higher reactivity, render them widely used in disease diagnosis, drug delivery, cosmetics and bioimaging, which has accelerated the development of nano industry (Pagano et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the large-scale production and commercialization of NPs have aroused increasing concerns about their environmental safety (Ruotolo et al., 2018), especially for agricultural products. NPs could potentially enter plants via accidental release, contaminated soil/sediment, and air emissions, thereby causing physiological effect and even inhibiting the normal growth (Gupta et al., 2017; Tombuloglu et al., 2020). Proverbially, plants are at the primary trophic level in the ecosystem and represent the beginning of the food chain of the majority of animals, including humans (Hatami et al., 2017). Hence, a good understanding of the uptake, translocation of NPs in plants, as well as plant physiological response to NPs exposure is critical for assessing the environmental risks as well as food safety.

A large number of studies have demonstrated that NPs could enter plants through pathways of root uptake and foliar uptake (Cao et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020), and the uptake, translocation process, as well as physiological effects were affected by various factors, such as surface charge and particle size of NPs, plant species, etc. (Spielman-Sun et al., 2017; Caldelas et al., 2020; L.L. Sun et al., 2020; X.D. Sun et al., 2020). For instance, Hu et al. (2020) observed that differently coated CeO2 NPs with the hydrodynamic size <20 nm could be effectively taken up by the cotton and maize leaves and translocated to the root compartments, and positively charged CeO2 NPs with zeta potential >15 mV had higher delivery efficiencies than negatively charged ones. To date, the majority of these studies concerning foliar uptake and mobility of NPs are using NPs based on metals or metal oxides, but little attention has been paid to the foliar uptake and translocation of other non-metallic NPs. At present, most of the published studies demonstrated that the main pathway for NPs entry into the leaf epidermis was through stomatal opening (Saebo et al., 2012; Hussain et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2020). However, a recent study by Avellan et al. (2019) showed that negatively charged citrate-coated Au NPs were observed to enter the leaves via stomata, while PVP-coated Au NPs crossed the cuticle layer. Overall, there still exists great knowledge gap in the uptake pathway in the context of differently coated NPs in various plants, meriting further investigation. As a rising star in the quantum dots family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) retain the advantages of graphene in addition to the above characteristics (Liu and Tang, 2020; Wu et al., 2021), and good biocompatibility and stability in aqueous solution are conducive to chemical functional modification to achieve the application purpose in different fields (Chakravarty et al., 2015). Our recent work (Sun et al., 2021) regarding the root uptake of CdS/ZnS QDs with different surface charge by maize showed that root uptake was an effective way for QDs entry into the plants, and the occurrence of aggregates limited the internalization and upward translocation. However, little effort has been devoted to examining the foliar uptake and translocation pathway of GQDs with different surface charge in plants.

The extraordinary properties of NPs effectively allow them to penetrate cells, causing varying degrees of nano-toxicity to plants (L.L. Sun et al., 2020; X.D. Sun et al., 2020), correspondingly, their potential harm to plants has aroused widespread concern. Most research on the response of plants to NPs exposure mainly focuses on two aspects, namely the variation in metabolomics and activities of antioxidant enzymes probing the induced oxidative stress, and the change in chlorophyll fluorescence parameters indicating its effect on photosynthesis (Keller et al., 2017; Ruotolo et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2019; Lennon et al., 2021). Cota-Ruiz et al. (2018) reported that 25 mg/L Cu (OH)2 NPs markedly upregulated the expression of the Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase (SOD) gene by 1.92-fold. Likewise, CdTe QDs significantly increased the activities of antioxidant enzymes in rice seedlings (Lian et al., 2019) and led to the decrease of chlorophyll fluorescence quantum yield by approximately 35% in white mustard (Modlitbova et al., 2020). However, there is still a critical knowledge gap regarding GQDs-related physiological effects on plants. Therefore, the objectives of the present work were to: 1) systematically understand the foliar uptake, occurrence on plant leaves, as well as leaf-to-root translocation of GQDs with different charge, 2) determine GQDs-related effects on the plant antioxidant system and photosynthesis and the relationship with surface charge.

NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs were purchased from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., (Nanjing, China). The morphology and particle size were characterized using an Ultra High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100) which was operated at 200 kV. Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) was used to monitor the zeta-potential of the GQDs. A pH analyzer (Twinno inno pH 300, Water Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd., Nantong, China) was applied to monitor the pH of the GQDs dispersions. A Nicolet 6700 spectrometer equipped with a deuterated sulfate glycine detector (DTGS/KBr) and a multifunctional attenuated total reflectance sampling accessory with a diamond wafer was used to obtain Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of the two GQDs, and the spectrum was recorded in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 using OMNIC 8.2. All of the other auxiliary reagents in the study were of analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Trustin Chemical Co., Ltd., China.

Seeds of maize (Zea mays L.) were purchased from Shanxi Qiangsheng Seed Co., Ltd., China. Firstly, the seeds were soaked in 10% H2O2 for 15 min and washed three times with distilled water for sterilization, then soaked in a petri dish containing distilled water till germination, during which the water was changed once a day. Next, they were cultivated in polyethylene pots filled with unpolluted soils that were collected from the experimental base of Shanxi University (Table S1). And then, they were transferred to MGC-450HP controlled climate chamber (Shanghai Yiheng Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China) for four weeks, under a cycle of 16 h illumination (105 mol/m2) at 26 °C, 8 h darkness at 24 °C, and 40–55% relative humidity (Sun et al., 2021). Four weeks later, healthy maize seedlings with uniform height (27 ± 0.4 cm) were selected for the following exposure experiment. Then the dust associated with the leaves was rinsed with deionized water three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of GQDs on maize leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the size of the fiber-optical probe, round “spot” with a diameter of 0.6 cm on the maize leaf surfaces was produced using the large circular end of a 5 mL pipette (Sun et al., 2016), which served as the determination unit in this work. Different dosage of NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs solutions were uniformly introduced onto these spots using a 10 μL flat-head microinjector (Zhejiang Lichen Instrument Technology Co., Ltd., China) to produce a concentration gradient (0, 100, 200, 400, 500, 600, 800 and 1000 ng/spot) on the maize leaves. After the distilled water evaporated completely, the fluorescence spectra of the two GQDs were scanned by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 2-m fiber-optic (Agilent, USA) in situ.

Individual GQDs stock solutions were diluted with distilled water to form a dispersion of 10 mg/L for foliar exposure. Fifteen seedlings reaching 3-leaf growth stage were selected and evenly divided into three groups, and each replicate containing 5 seedlings were used as the control group, NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs exposure group, respectively. Subsequently, the two GQDs dispersion were separately and evenly sprayed onto the adaxial side of three leaves per plants once a day with a 10-mL ultra-fine spray bottle over an 8-day period, with a total volume of 80 mL. Using the same protocol, the control group was sprayed with distilled water without GQDs. To prevent GQDs from spraying onto the stem and cultivation soils, a plastic partition containing a circular aperture was made with the pore diameter (1 cm) equal to the thickness of the stem, which was larger than the area of the pots (Fig. S1). When being sprayed, it was sheathed at the joint between the leaf and stem. The experimental conditions during exposure were the same as cultivation. During the exposure period, 50 mL of tap water should be added every day to ensure that the seedlings were not disturbed by drought.

After sufficient interaction, leaf, stem, and root samples at different time points (1, 2, 5 and 8 days) were collected for the determination of GQDs occurrence and distribution. According to the method by Zhao et al. (2017) with a slight modification, all fresh samples were immediately prefixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and vacuum-preserved for 12 min, then fixed at 4 °C for another 2.5 h. After being washed three times with Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) (pH = 7.2), the samples were transferred to 2% (v/v) glutaraldehyde and stored overnight at 4 °C. The next day, these samples were soaked by sequential immersion in different concentrations of ethanol (30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90% and 100%) before treatment with epoxy resin. Subsequently, the samples were sliced with a sterile knife and collected Superfrost Plus glass slides covered with cover glasses. Fluorescence images of all samples were obtained under an Olympus BX61 fluorescence microscope (FM) equipped with DPT1 digital micro-CCD, BX-UCB fluorescence exciter (Olympus, Japan), which exposure time was 0.4 s and the image size was 2040 × 1536 pixels. Nine microscopic images per sample were captured at each exposure time point. To gain better understanding about the pathway of GQDs entry into the blade, GQDs distribution on the leaf surface after 8 days of exposure were measured by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 200, FEI) operated at 15 kV in low vacuum (133 Pa). Before SEM analysis, the leaf samples were dried in an oven at 45 °C for 1.5 days (Larue et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses of chlorophyll paraments in maize leaves
ce:section.ce:para: At pre-set time (1, 2, 5 and 8 days), the stress levels of NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs on photosynthetic organs of maize were evaluated by analyzing the main chlorophyll fluorescence parameters on a FluorCam open chlorophyll fluorescence imaging system (Beijing Ecotech Co., Ltd., China) which furnished with four LED light source boards with adjustable angle and height, a height-adjustable CCD lens, bracket, and control unit. The imaging size was 20 × 20 cm. All measured seedlings must undergo dark adaptation for at least 15 min to transfer all the electrons accumulated at the plastoquinone, ensure all the reaction centers of photosystem II (PSII) were in an open state (Zhao et al., 2018). Then, the chlorophyll fluorescence images and parameters comprising basal chlorophyll fluorescence (F0), the ratio of chlorophyll fluorescence decrease (Rfd), maximum quantum yield (Fv/Fm), photochemical quenching (qP), and non-photochemical quenching (NPQ) of the intact leaves were obtained at room temperature and analyzed on the Fluor Cam imaging software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Induction of oxidation stress by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The biochemical parameters of leaf tissues were measured at day 2 and 8, and those of stem and root tissues were detected at day 8. The tissues (0.1 g) were accurately weighed respectively, and ground in a mortar containing 0.9 mL 50 mmol/L pre-cooled PBS (pH = 7.8). The tissue homogenate was immediately transferred to a 4-mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 3500 r/min for 10 min in HC-3018 High speed centrifuge (Anhui USTC Zonkia Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China), the supernatants were collected for the determination of biochemical indexes. Then, the content of malondialdehyde (MDA, A003-1-2), as well as the enzyme activities of SOD (A001-3-2), peroxidase (POD, A084-3-1) and catalase (CAT, A007-1-1), were measured with a 722 N visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Precision and Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., China) by corresponding assay kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China).

For all measured items, the final data were the mean value of at least nine replicates and were shown as mean ± standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA, P < 0.05) tests was applied to evaluate the significance of differences. Student's t-test with a 95% confidence level was used to evaluate whether the accumulation of GQDs in maize tissues was significantly different compared with the control group.

The morphology and particle size displayed in HRTEM images (Fig. S2A and B) are highly consistent in dimension for NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs with the particle size of 13.0 ± 1.8 nm and 14.1 ± 2.3 nm, respectively. And, the FTIR spectra confirmed the presence of amino and hydroxy groups on the GQDs surfaces (Fig. S2C and D). The characteristic peaks of NH2-GQDs appeared at 602, 1653 and 3260 cm−1, corresponding to the vibrations of NH, CO, and NH, respectively, while the peaks of OH-GQDs located at 1037, 1653 and 3349 cm−1, were attributed to the vibrations of OH, CH, and OH, respectively. The zeta potential of NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs suspension at 100 mg/L were measured with the values of +25.9 ± 1.3 mV and −11.6 ± 2.6 mV, respectively. Commonly, a zeta potential lower than −10 mV is considered to anionic, while greater than +10 mV to be cationic (Lowry et al., 2016). The pH of NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs dispersions in distilled water were 7.20 and 6.88, respectively. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of two GQDs dispersions within 8 days were scanned under their respective optimum excitation and emission wavelengths (Fig. S2E and F). Obviously, the emission peaks and photoluminescence intensity of the two GQDs had no significant change, indicating that GQDs had good stability and dispersibility in aqueous solutions (Lian et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of GQDs on maize leaves
ce:section.ce:para: As recorded in our recent study (Sun et al., 2021), the fiber-optic fluorimetry achieved the in situ indication of the variation in CdS/ZnS QDs occurrence on the root surfaces of maize plants. To verify feasibility of this approach for the measurement of GQDs associated with the maize leaf surfaces, the fluorescent response to the concentration of GQDs in the range of 0–1000 ng/spot were determined under the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of NH2-GQDs (326 nm/415 nm) and OH-GQDs (300 nm/393 nm). When measuring the GQDs on the leaf surfaces, the interfering factor that should be considered is the autofluorescence of chlorophyll in maize leaves. Fortunately, the fluorescence spectrum of maize leaves without GQDs treatment shows that the chlorophyll fluorescence response obtained at the optimal excitation wavelengths of the two GQDs is extremely weak, and there is no interference to the measurement of GQDs (Fig. 1A and C, 0 ng/spot). As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-GQDs retained on the maize leaf surfaces strongly depends on the concentration and shows a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9994). Similar results were also obtained for OH-GQDs (Fig. 1C and D). To display the accuracy and credibility of this method, the analysis merits are shown in Table S2. The excellent analytical performance further demonstrates the achievement of the fiber-optic fluorimetry in probing the variation in GQDs content associated with the maize leaves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and translocation of GQDs in maize seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: To gain more insight into the uptake and downward translocation of GQDs with different charge in the maize seedlings, the FM images of GQDs located at maize leaves, stems and roots within 8 days were captured. As evidenced in Fig. 2A, the accumulation of both GQDs on the leaf surfaces, including number and aggregation degree, displayed in a time-dependent increasing manner. Specifically, a remarkable increase in average particle size for NH2-GQDs from 15.1 ± 3.2 nm at day 1 to 102.8 ± 5.6 nm at day 8, and for OH-GQDs from 13.7 ± 1.8 nm to 74.2 ± 3.4 nm (P < 0.05), indicating the occurrence of observable GQDs aggregation on the leaf surfaces. Similarly, our recent study also found the aggregation of differently coated CdS/ZnS QDs on the plant root surfaces, and the presence of aggregation was dominated by the interaction of QDs coating with plant cell wall via the formation of hydrogen bond (Sun et al., 2021). Meanwhile, the distribution levels of NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs increased by 6.6 and 3.5 times per cm2, respectively (P < 0.05). Meanwhile, the above observation could get confirmed by the variation in fluorescence spectra of GQDs. The fluorescence responses of both GQDs exhibited a strong time-dependent trend. Specifically, compared with their initial values, NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs undergo an increase in the fluorescence intensity by 34.7% and 11.6%, and a red shift of 69 nm and 56 nm, respectively, after 8 days of exposure (Fig. 2B and C), which provided a more quantitative evidence that NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs exhibited differential accumulation and aggregation on the leaf surfaces.

Statistically, NH2-GQDs with positive charge had relatively higher accumulation and aggregation degree on the leaf surfaces than OH-GQDs with negative charge (P < 0.05), which was consistent with the observation regarding the effects of surface charge on the foliar accumulation of metallic nanoparticles (Avellan et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020). The observed difference in association between differently functionalized GQDs could be explained by that the electrostatic attraction between NH2-GQDs (+) and plant cell wall (−) favored the higher uptake rate and accumulation of NH2-GQDs relative to OH-GQDs. From SEM measurements (Fig. S3), both GQDs mainly appeared in and near the stomata, while a very small part was internalized into epicuticular wax at day 8, suggesting that the main pathway for GQDs reaching the plant vasculature is the stomatal opening. The same internalization pathway was also reported for CuO and Ag NPs internalization into cabbage and lettuce leaves, respectively (Larue et al., 2014; Xiong et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQDs on photosynthesis of maize seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Both GQDs showed a significant inhibitory effect on the growth phenotype of maize seedlings (Fig. S4 and Table S3). After 8 days, root elongation of maize seedlings treated with NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs were (18.5 ± 0.2) % and (35.1 ± 0.6) % lower than that of the control group, respectively. Meanwhile, the NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs treatments decreased the fresh weight of seedlings by (11.8 ± 0.2) % and (29.3 ± 0.4) %, respectively. Overall, these observations revealed that these two GQDs treatment caused phenotype alterations to the maize plants and the impact varied significantly with the surface charge (P < 0.05).

Fv/Fm, representing the effective quantum yield of photochemical energy conversion in PSII, provides a reliable and rapid method for evaluating external stress (Antonoglou et al., 2018). The optimum Fv/Fm ratio for healthy higher plants is between 0.8 and 0.85 (Woo et al., 2008). From Fig. 5B and C, after 8 days of foliar treatment by NH2-GQDs and OH-GQDs, the Fv/Fm ratio decreased by 0.32 and 0.36, respectively (P < 0.05). This indicated that the contribution of GQDs to the dysfunction of PSII system was accompanied by the decline in the utilization efficiency of light energy by the maize seedlings, which inevitably led to the decrease of photosynthesis efficiency (Woo et al., 2008). A similar time-dependent decreasing trend was also observed in qP with the maximum reduction value reaching 0.96 at day 8 with GQDs treatments (P < 0.05) (Fig. 5). While, contrary to the decrease of qP, the NPQ value increased significantly, suggesting that GQDs induced the increase of energy dissipation by the PSII in the form of heat. Thus, the part used for photochemical reactions decreased, which indicated the maize seedlings could protected PSII from the damage by increasing appropriate heat dissipation (Moustakas et al., 2017). Still from Fig. 5, Rfd values also showed a continuous reduction with the prolonged exposure time, suggesting that the injury to photosynthetic organs were gradually deepened (Modlitbova et al., 2020). Overall, the negatively charged OH-GQDs had relatively stronger influence on the chlorophyll fluorescence parameters than positively charged NH2-GQDs, suggesting that the former had more serious adverse effects on photosynthesis than the latter. Hence, this observation might explain the differences in phenotype alterations caused by differently coated GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of GQDs on the activity of antioxidant enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: As a product of lipid peroxidation, MDA can be used to indicate the damage of cell membrane caused by free radicals caused by oxidative stress (Hatami et al., 2017). Compared with the control plants, GQDs treatment significantly increased MDA content in all maize tissues after 8 days of exposure, irrespective of the surface charge (Fig. 7A). Presumably, the observed difference in the increase of MDA content in different tissues (leaf > stem > root) might be explained by the distinct GQDs accumulation in different tissues, first in leaves, second in stems, and least in roots. For the same tissue, GQDs-induced MDA increase was variable with different surface charge (P < 0.05). For example, after 8 days of treatment with positively charged NH2-GQDs and negatively charged OH-GQDs, the MDA content in the leaves increased by 2.9 and 4.3 times, respectively (Fig. 7A). However, as mentioned above, the number of dispersed particles and aggregates of NH2-GQDs on the leaves exceeded that of OH-GQDs. Logically, the GQDs-induced effects on MDA content was not only related to their enrichment, but also the functional groups (Zhou et al., 2017).

SOD, CAT and POD play an important role in scavenging ROS (O2 −, H2O2 and ·OH) by transforming into non-toxic H2O or O2 in their respective ways (Tarrahi et al., 2018). From Fig. 7B, the activities of SOD, CAT and POD in the leaves increased significantly after 2 or 8 days of treatment with the two GQDs. Similarly, a study by Zhang et al. (2021) demonstrated that GQDs treatment increased SOD activity in lettuce seedlings dramatically. Accordingly, the increased activities of the three enzymes accompanied by the activation of self-defense system suggested that GQDs caused significant oxidative stress to the maize plants. Notably, the variation in the activities of the three antioxidant enzymes exhibited significant differences with differently coated GQDs. Specifically, the increase in enzymatic activity induced by negatively charged OH-GQDs is approximately 1.6 times than that of positively charged NH2-GQDs (Fig. 7B), suggesting that OH-GQDs produced relatively stronger oxidative stress to the maize leaves relative to NH2-GQDs. In contrast, compared with the control group, there was almost no significant change in the activities of the three antioxidant enzymes in the stems and roots after 8 days of GQDs treatment, regardless of surface charge (Fig. S5). Reasonably, the non-enzymatic system may play a more important role in regulating the antioxidant balance in the stems and roots of seedlings treated with GQDs (Tarrahi et al., 2018; Moradbeygi et al., 2020).

The present study provided direct evidence that the surface charge has a significant impact on uptake, translocation, and physiological response of GQDs in maize seedlings. The positively charged NH2-GQDs had greater accumulation and aggregation than negatively charged OH-GQDs in the leaves. The GQDs mainly penetrate the leaves via the stomata, subsequently move down to the stems and roots through the phloem along with the flux of photosynthate, irrespective of surface charge. With the passage of exposure time, the aggregates with increasing particle sizes will hinder the shoot-to-root transmission of GQDs along the phloem. The foliar exposure to GQDs could impair the photosynthesis by damaging to photosynthetic tissues, thereby disrupting the normal growth of plants. In comparison, OH-GQDs induced a stronger inhibitory effect on plant photosynthesis relative to NH2-GQDs. The information provided in this work will help us better understand the behavior of GQDs in crops.

We have made substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work, as well as the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of experimental results for this study; We have drafted the work or revised it critically for important intellectual content; We have approved the final version to be published; We agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.
All persons who have made substantial contributions to the present study have been reported in the manuscript and have given their written permission to be named.

Haifeng Sun: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing-Reviewing and Editing; Meng Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing-original draft; Jing Wang: Investigation, Methodology; Weipeng Wang: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 42177215), and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (201901D11103).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.151506.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Antonoglou et al., 2018
